summary: This is a very short teaser post where Joel Spolsky introduces a revised excerpt from his book 'User Interface Design for Programmers.' He uses dramatic examples from aviation safety to hook readers into thinking about usability design. The post frames usability as a life-or-death concern, using the concept of CFIT (Controlled Flight Into Terrain) to illustrate how poor interface design has real consequences. Joel notes he is in the process of extensively rewriting and expanding his earlier book on UI design.
key_points: Poor usability in critical systems like aircraft controls can directly cause fatal accidents (CFIT)
Usability failures exist on a spectrum of severity, from fatal crashes to minor injuries to mere inconvenience
Joel was actively revising and expanding his book 'User Interface Design for Programmers' at the time of writing
money_quotes: Bad usability in the design of aircraft controls can result in what is cheerfully referred to as CFIT: Controlled Flight Into Terrain.
If you're lucky, the mistakes you make in usability design will merely cause people to lose limbs, or, heck, even just thumbs. No biggie!
key_insight: Usability design has real-world consequences that range from inconvenience to catastrophe, and treating it as an afterthought is dangerous.
